Proof Gates
On March 9, 2026, AMIE’s urgent-care study and ECRI’s top safety concern made accuracy the opening bid, not the rollout gate.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Google AMIE’s 90% Diagnosis Result Has a Cost Problem: On March 9, 2026, AMIE’s urgent-care study and ECRI’s top safety concern made accuracy the opening bid, not the rollout gate. Image created by the author with diffusion-synthesis and Python post-processing.At Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, AMIE was not a demo on a slide. It was a text-chat window taking urgent-care histories from 100 adults while a physician watched for the failure that matters most: approval based on accuracy alone turns liability from a benchmark-table score into an exam-room problem. One visible miss can mean patient harm, audit scrutiny, and a rollout locked in place.
For healthcare AI founders, CMIOs, clinical product leaders, and hospital operators, the real object is the pre-visit transcript, the differential diagnosis, the management plan, and the human signature still carrying the consequence. On March 9, 2026, Google’s AMIE feasibility study landed beside ECRI’s 2026 top patient-safety concern, Navigating the AI Diagnostic Dilemma.
The chat window arrived with a chaperone.
That same-day split is the story. AMIE looked clinically useful in a real urgent-care setting, but ECRI warned that unchecked dependence on AI diagnosis can increase errors, perpetuate bias, and erode clinical thinking. Both…
